kpi_cards.label: LME CASH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 17-Jul OFFICIAL (Westmetall) - still the latest PUBLISHED official at this ~06:05 London refresh on Tue 21-Jul; Westmetall had not yet posted the 20-Jul settlement. The 20-Jul session itself traded softer (aluminium about $3,138/t, -0.85%, Trading Economics). Backdrop escalated again on Monday: Iran said it disabled two oil tankers attempting to transit Hormuz without permission, a third US service member was confirmed killed since Friday, and Iran struck Kuwaiti power and desalination sites plus Bahrain, home of the US 5th Fleet. Brent topped $90/bbl.

kpi_cards.label: LME 3-MONTH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 17-Jul official, -$20.00 (-0.63%) vs 16-Jul. The LME's day-delayed 3M closing print for the same session was $3,185.00 (+1.11%), so the curve closed firmer than the ring settled. Resistance $3,230 (MA20) then $3,260 (.382 retracement); support the early-July low near $3,085.

kpi_cards.label: CASH-3M ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: BACKWARDATION on the latest published official. Prompt tightness persists with LME stock at 280,100 t (lowest since 2022, about -45% YTD). Watch whether Monday's tanker interdictions steepen the front of the curve at the next official.

kpi_cards.label: LME STOCK (t)
kpi_cards.delta: -1,500 t on the 17-Jul official; roughly -45% YTD from 509,250 t on 02-Jan-2026. Lowest since 2022 and still drawing. SHFE stocks are falling in parallel - a genuinely tight physical picture on both sides of the arb.

benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: 17-Jul OFFICIAL (Westmetall) $3,154.00 vs 16-Jul $3,170.00 = -$16.00 (-0.50%). This remains the LATEST PUBLISHED official: Westmetall had not yet posted the 20-Jul official at this ~06:05 London refresh (LME officials print 13:20 London and are republished with a lag). Separately, the 20-Jul session traded lower - see the metals board. avg = June-2026 monthly mean ($3,458.6).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 17-Jul official $3,146.50 vs 16-Jul $3,166.50 (-$20.00, -0.63%) - latest published official. The LME's own day-delayed 3M CLOSING price for 17-Jul printed $3,185.00 (+1.11%), above the official, i.e. the afternoon curve was firmer than the ring. Resistance stack: $3,230 (MA20), then $3,260 (.382 of the June-July decline). avg = June-2026 3M mean ($3,438.9).

benchmark.name: Cash-3M spread
benchmark.note: BACKWARDATION at +$7.50 (cash $3,154.00 over 3M $3,146.50) on the 17-Jul official - the latest published curve. Positive cash-3M = backwardation = prompt tightness, consistent with LME stocks at 280,100 t, the lowest since 2022. The next official print is the first chance to see whether the tanker interdictions widen it.

benchmark.name: LME stock (t)
benchmark.note: 280,100 t at the 17-Jul official, down 1,500 t on the day and roughly -45% year-to-date from 509,250 t on 02-Jan-2026. Lowest since 2022. Draws have been continuous through July; SHFE stockpiles are also declining.

premiums.name: MJP Q3 (Main Japanese Port)
premiums.src: Q3-2026 SETTLED at $395/t (S&P Global Platts, assessed 06-Jul-2026) - a decade high, roughly +12% q/q vs Q2's $350-353/t. Q4 negotiations open around September against this anchor; the swing variable is whether Hormuz disruption persists at current intensity. Source: Platts via IndexBox. avg = Q1-26 settlement ($195).

premiums.name: CIF Japan spot (MJP spot)
premiums.src: Spot firming toward the Q3 settlement: Platts CIF Japan spot last publicly at ~$370/t (06-Jul); SMM's 'squeeze risk climbs under LME deep backwardation' analysis flags tight spot supply pushing the QMJP premium up. Point ~$365/t. Verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0343 / SMM weekly.

premiums.name: European Duty Unpaid (EDU)
premiums.src: Platts P1020A duty-UNPAID in-warehouse Rotterdam assessed $525-560/t (mid $542.50) on 03-Jul-2026, down from $600-625/t on 13-May as the Middle East war premium faded. No fresher public print. Structural floor: 6% EU duty plus CBAM plus idled European capacity. Liquidity thin - Rotterdam competes with Asia for units.

premiums.name: Billets Rotterdam Duty-Paid
premiums.src: Value-added 6063 billet conversion premium, in-whs duty-paid Rotterdam — last public reference (~late-Jun; no fresh public print). Conversion premia are stickier than ingot premia; Mozal remains down. Verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0002.

premiums.name: MW US Transaction (Platts)
premiums.src: Platts US MW Transaction premium last publicly assessed 108.7¢/lb ≈ $2,396/t (03-Jul). The all-in US delivered price embeds the 50% Section-232 tariff (≈$1,577/t at the 17-Jul cash of $3,154), so the policy leg dominates. Alcoa's Q2 flagged Section-232 on Canadian metal rising ~$35m q/q. Verify S&P Global.

premiums.name: US Midwest Duty-Paid (P1020A)
premiums.src: SMM weekly DDP print 109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul). Duty-paid = LME + Section-232 tariff (50%) + market premium; the policy leg is ~two-thirds of the all-in. The tariff-$ component eased ~$8/t with Friday's softer LME. Verify Platts/Fastmarkets.

macro.name: Brent crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: Topped $90/bbl on Monday as US strikes on Iran entered a ninth consecutive day and Iran disabled two tankers in the Strait of Hormuz. Energy is the largest smelter cost line; European and Asian smelters carry the most exposure through the gas channel.

macro.name: US Dollar Index (DXY)
macro.note: DXY ~100.9, easing from early-July highs near 101.4 on softer labour-market signals and steadier Treasury yields. A softer dollar removes a headwind for metals even as the geopolitical risk premium re-widens.

macro.name: EUR/USD
macro.note: Euro ~1.14, holding near recent levels. The ECB still carries a war-energy inflation problem with Brent above $90 and European gas exposed to any further Gulf escalation.

macro.name: USD/CNY
macro.note: Yuan steady ~6.77. China's Q2 GDP grew 4.3% y/y — below the 4.5% consensus and the weakest since Q4-2022 — with June retail sales +1.0% and industrial output +5.3% (both beat) but H1 fixed-asset investment −5.7% y/y, the lowest since May-2020: production/export strength, deep investment weakness, keeping the record export valve open.

macro.name: USD/JPY
macro.note: Yen ~162.4, little changed. A still-weak yen cushions Japan-bound cargo premia in local terms; MJP Q4 talks (~Sep) open against a decade-high $395 anchor and a further-escalated Gulf conflict.

macro.name: US 10Y yield (%)
macro.note: 10Y ~4.56%, little changed after last week's cool inflation prints. Real rates remain the macro cap on metals; the swing risk this week is geopolitical (oil-driven inflation pass-through) rather than the Fed.

macro_events: HORMUZ MOVES TO ENFORCEMENT: Iran said on Monday 20-Jul that it disabled two oil tankers which attempted to transit the Strait of Hormuz without its permission. This is the first direct interdiction of individual merchant vessels in the conflict and converts what had been a de-facto shipping stoppage into an actively enforced one. The Gulf accounts for roughly 9% of global primary aluminium supply. (NPR)

US CASUALTIES AND STRIKE TEMPO: A third US service member was confirmed killed in operations since Friday, and US strikes on Iran ran to a ninth consecutive day following the collapse of talks over a Strait of Hormuz memorandum of understanding. Iran struck electricity and water desalination installations in Kuwait and targeted Bahrain, home of the US Navy's 5th Fleet. (NPR)
ENERGY: Brent topped $90/bbl on Monday. Sustained crude and gas strength raises smelter operating costs and biases marginal European and Asian capacity toward curtailment rather than restart.

ALUMINA RELIEF RAMPING: EGA's Al Taweelah refinery has been at full alumina production since 10-Jul, targeting 50% of plant capacity within days of restart and full capability by end-2026. This is the principal counterweight to the geopolitical supply risk and helps explain the flat price drifting lower despite the escalation. (EGA)

US TRADE POLICY: The Section 232 aluminium tariff remains at 50% on the full customs value for products made entirely or almost entirely of aluminium, under the proclamation signed 01-Jun-2026 effective 08-Jun-2026 through 31-Dec-2027, with CBP implementation guidance issued 05-Jun-2026. This is the single largest administered component of the US duty-paid premium. (CBP)
lme_commentary: The 17-Jul official remains the latest PUBLISHED LME official (cash $3,154.00 / 3M $3,146.50 / stock 280,100 t): Westmetall had not yet posted the 20-Jul settlement at this ~06:05 London refresh on Tue 21-Jul, and LME officials print at 13:20 London before being republished with a lag. The 20-Jul session did trade, and it traded softer - aluminium settled around $3,138/t, down 0.85%, giving back part of the rebound from the early-July four-month low near $3,085 (Trading Economics). Copper's LME day-delayed 3M close for 20-Jul was $13,525.50, down 0.54%. The softness sits awkwardly against the geopolitical tape. On Monday Iran said it disabled two oil tankers that attempted to transit the Strait of Hormuz without its permission - the first time individual merchant vessels have been directly interdicted in this conflict, converting a de-facto stoppage into an enforced one. A third US service member was confirmed killed since Friday, US strikes on Iran ran to a ninth consecutive day, and Iran targeted Kuwaiti electricity and water desalination installations as well as Bahrain, home of the US Navy's 5th Fleet. Brent topped $90/bbl. The counterweight is real: EGA's Al Taweelah refinery resumed full alumina production on 10-Jul and is ramping toward full capability by end-2026, which is the most plausible explanation for why the flat price is drifting rather than spiking. Structurally the market remains tight - LME stock at 280,100 t is the lowest since 2022 and roughly 45% below the start of the year, SHFE stocks are drawing, and the cash-3M curve is in backwardation at +$7.50. The next official print is the first clean read on whether the tanker interdictions steepen the front of the curve.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: $328/t FOB Western Australia (SMM, 09-Jul) — down from ~$338 as the EGA Al Taweelah refinery restart (10-Jul) ramps and the ex-China surplus deepens. The SMM Alumina Index eased to ¥2,730.17/t (−¥12.8 w/w). Gulf-routed cargoes still carry elevated war-risk cover given the further-escalated Kuwaiti energy-infrastructure strikes. Verify SMM/Fastmarkets.

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region ~$303/t — easing on the building surplus (Q1 avg $306.9; latest public reference, no fresher print surfaced). SMM flags ex-China metallurgical-grade output down ~6% y/y in June — a partial offset to the EGA restart. Verify Platts.

alumina.name: SHFE-region alumina (SMM Alumina Index)
alumina.src: SMM Alumina Index ¥2,730.17/t, down ¥12.8/t week-on-week; regional spot Shandong ¥2,710–2,780, Henan ¥2,730–2,800, Shanxi ¥2,750–2,800, Guangxi ¥2,600–2,680, Guizhou ¥2,760–2,800. Futures trade below moving averages with port stocks building even as the Gulf strait squeeze still weighs on regional refinery feed (~60% of Gulf alumina imports transit Hormuz).

alumina.name: Platts FOB Australia (futures)
alumina.src: Alumina FOB Australia (Platts) futures ~$308/t — CME/TradingView; no material move in the latest public print.

alumina_notes: The alumina market remains the mirror image of metal: globally LOOSENING (EGA's 2.4 Mtpa restart ramping since 10-Jul, Guinea bauxite oversupply, Chinese port stocks building; SMM Alumina Index down to ¥2,730.17/t, −¥12.8 w/w) — ex-China consolidation has stepped down toward the $300–330 band (FOB Australia now $328, from ~$338).

The regional wrinkle has widened further overnight: a 9th consecutive night of US strikes on Iran, a second consecutive Iranian strike on Kuwait's Shuaiba desalination/power complex, and Strait transits down to as few as ~3 vessels/24h all re-price the war-risk cover embedded in the ~60% of Gulf alumina imports that transit Hormuz. SMM separately flags ex-China metallurgical-grade output down ~6% y/y in June.

Guinea's pending export-quota policy (a considered cap near 150 Mt for 2026, vs 178 Mt shipped in 2025, targeting >$100/dmt bauxite) remains undecreed as of end-June and is the one clear bullish catalyst on the commodity leg; record Australian and Metro Mining shipments underline how long the seaborne bauxite market currently is.
net_read: ENFORCED BLOCKADE MEETS A SOFTER TAPE. Monday's session traded aluminium down to about $3,138/t (-0.85%) even as Iran moved from threatening the Strait of Hormuz to physically disabling two tankers that tried to cross it without permission. That divergence is the story: the market is discounting escalation because the only credible supply relief in the system - EGA's Al Taweelah alumina restart, at full production since 10-Jul and ramping to full capability by end-2026 - is arriving at the same time. The structural picture has not loosened: LME stock is 280,100 t on the latest published official, the lowest since 2022 and roughly -45% YTD, SHFE inventories are falling, and cash-3M holds a +$7.50 backwardation. Brent above $90/bbl keeps smelter power costs pointed the wrong way. The asymmetry favours the upside on any interruption to the Al Taweelah ramp or any widening of the tanker interdictions beyond crude into general merchant traffic.

feed.text: HORMUZ ENFORCEMENT: Iran said it disabled two oil tankers that attempted to transit the Strait of Hormuz without its permission - the first direct interdiction of individual merchant vessels in this conflict, moving the strait from de-facto stoppage to enforced closure (NPR).

US CASUALTIES: A third US service member was confirmed killed in military operations since Friday. US strikes on Iran ran to a ninth consecutive day since the Hormuz memorandum-of-understanding talks collapsed (NPR).

REGIONAL SPILLOVER: Iran targeted electricity installations and water desalination plants in Kuwait, which hosts a large US military presence, and also struck Bahrain, home of the US Navy's 5th Fleet. Iran's stated posture is to respond in kind to US strikes (NPR).
ENERGY: Brent topped $90/bbl as the conflict intensified. Energy is the single largest smelter operating cost line, and European and Asian smelters are the most exposed to the gas-price channel.

FLAT PRICE SOFTER: The 20-Jul session traded aluminium down to about $3,138/t (-0.85%), giving back part of the rebound from the early-July four-month low near $3,085. LME copper's day-delayed 3M close was $13,525.50 (-0.54%) (Trading Economics; LME).

SUPPLY RELIEF: EGA resumed full alumina production at Al Taweelah on 10-Jul after a three-and-a-half-month outage, targeting 50% of plant capacity within days and full capability by end-2026. The refinery produced 2.4 Mt of alumina in 2025, close to half of EGA's requirement (EGA).

news.headline: Iran disabled two oil tankers attempting to transit the Strait of Hormuz without permission on Monday - the first direct interdiction of individual merchant vessels in this conflict. Combined with a third US service member killed since Friday, a ninth consecutive day of US strikes, and Iranian attacks on Kuwaiti power/desalination sites and Bahrain (US 5th Fleet), the strait has moved from blockade-by-threat to blockade-by-enforcement.

EGA's Al Taweelah refinery resumed full alumina production on 10-Jul after a three-and-a-half-month outage, targeting 50% of capacity within days and full capability by end-2026. At 2.4 Mt in 2025 it supplies close to half of EGA's alumina need - the only material supply-side relief currently visible and the most plausible reason the flat price is drifting rather than spiking.
news.source: Emirates Global Aluminium; The National; Reuters (10-Jul-2026)

news.headline: LME stocks stand at 280,100 t on the latest published official, the lowest since 2022 and roughly 45% below the 509,250 t start-of-year level, with SHFE stockpiles also declining. Cash-3M holds a +$7.50 backwardation - prompt tightness rather than surplus.

Brent topped $90/bbl on Monday as the conflict intensified. Power is the largest single smelter cost line; sustained energy strength compresses marginal-producer margins in Europe and Asia and raises the incentive to curtail rather than restart capacity.

Norsk Hydro reports Q2 2026 tomorrow, 22-Jul at 07:00 CEST, with a webcast at 08:30 CEST - the first European producer P&L covering a full quarter of the disruption regime, and the cleanest public read on how European smelter power costs and regional premiums are flowing through margins.

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.97bn (+24% q/q, highest in nearly a decade); adj. EPS $2.12 (MISS vs ~$2.31 consensus); adj. net income $562m; adj. EBITDA $901m (from $595m in Q1); Aluminum segment RECORD EBITDA $1.073bn at 32.3% margin.
earnings.readthrough: First producer P&L under the Hormuz-disruption regime: record aluminium margins and near-decade-high revenue on higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums, but adj. EPS missed on higher costs — notably ~$35m more Section-232 tariff on Canadian metal into the US — and the shares fell. South32 upstream acquisition ($4.1bn upfront, up to $5.6bn contingent to 2030) progressing, close expected H1-2027. The tariff is a double edge: it inflates US premia but taxes Alcoa's cross-border Canadian metal.

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due Wed 22-Jul (07:00 CEST) — the next European producer read on power costs and premium realisation.

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.headline: H1 copper-equivalent output +3% y/y; aluminium division improved; strong bauxite recovery; FY aluminium guidance UNCHANGED at 3.25–3.45 Mt; Pilbara Q2 shipments 89 Mt (+5%).
earnings.readthrough: First upstream print of the Q2 season (this morning): Western volumes are intact — the Gulf disruption is expressing itself in prices, freight and premia rather than output. Kitimat/AP60 ramp continues; H1 financial results due 13-Aug.

earnings.headline: Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady.
earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.headline: Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%).
earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings.headline: Guides H1-26 net profit ¥1.8–1.95bn, +154.4–175.6% YoY.
earnings.readthrough: Credits industry-chain cost reduction, aluminium prices fluctuating at highs, high-value-added deep-processing mix and energy-saving retrofits — the first sizeable China H1 pre-announcement of the season and a positive read-across for Chinese producer margins.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly and episodic; entries show each listed peer's most recent reported results as of 21-Jul (public, listed-company information). No new peer releases since Alcoa on 16-Jul. Q2 season is open: Alcoa posted record Aluminum-segment EBITDA ($1.073bn, 32.3% margin) and near-decade-high revenue ($3.97bn) but missed on adjusted EPS ($2.12 vs roughly $2.31 consensus) on higher costs including around $35m of additional Section-232 tariff on Canadian metal, and also announced a $4.7bn acquisition. Rio Tinto's 15-Jul production report showed volumes intact with aluminium guidance unchanged at 3.25-3.45 Mt. NEXT: Norsk Hydro Q2 tomorrow, Wed 22-Jul at 07:00 CEST with a webcast at 08:30 CEST - the first European producer P&L covering a full quarter of the disruption regime and the cleanest public read on European smelter power costs and regional premium pass-through.

commercial.text: The 20-Jul session traded aluminium to about $3,138/t (-0.85%) while the latest published LME official remains 17-Jul (cash $3,154.00 / 3M $3,146.50). The notable feature is the divergence between a softening flat price and an escalating supply-risk backdrop - Monday's tanker interdictions are not yet in any official print. Resistance $3,230 (MA20) then $3,260; support the early-July low near $3,085.

Cash-3M backwardation at +$7.50 with LME stock at 280,100 t, the lowest since 2022 and roughly -45% YTD, and SHFE stocks also drawing. Prompt tightness is genuine on both sides of the arb. The next official print is the first clean read on whether enforcement at Hormuz steepens the front of the curve.

The three regions have decoupled on different logic: Japan's Q3 MJP settled at a decade-high $395/t on scarcity, Rotterdam duty-unpaid has faded to a $542.50/t mid as the war premium receded toward a structural 6%-duty-plus-CBAM floor, and the US duty-paid premium is dominated by the 50% Section 232 rate - roughly $1,575/t of administered cost at current LME. Watch Q4 MJP talks opening around September.

Brent above $90/bbl keeps the largest smelter cost line pointed the wrong way, with European and Asian producers most exposed through gas. On the other side, EGA's Al Taweelah alumina ramp is the first genuine easing in the raw-material chain since March and is the main reason the flat price is not spiking.

Norsk Hydro Q2 tomorrow (22-Jul, 07:00 CEST). Beyond that: the pace of the Al Taweelah ramp toward 50% and then full capability, whether tanker interdictions extend beyond crude into general merchant traffic, and whether LME stocks keep drawing below 280,100 t.

bottom_line: On 21-Jul the benchmark table still carries the 17-Jul LME official (cash $3,154.00, 3M $3,146.50, cash-3M +$7.50 backwardation, stock 280,100 t, about -45% YTD) because Westmetall had not published the 20-Jul settlement at this ~06:05 London refresh. The 20-Jul session itself traded lower - aluminium around $3,138/t, -0.85% - despite a materially worse geopolitical backdrop: Iran disabled two oil tankers attempting to transit the Strait of Hormuz without permission, the first direct interdiction of individual merchant vessels in this conflict; a third US service member was confirmed killed since Friday; US strikes ran to a ninth consecutive day; and Iran struck Kuwaiti power and desalination infrastructure and Bahrain, home of the US 5th Fleet. Brent topped $90/bbl. The offsetting force is EGA's Al Taweelah alumina refinery, at full production since 10-Jul and ramping toward full capability by end-2026. Net: a tight physical market with the lowest LME stocks since 2022 and a backwarded curve, temporarily balanced by visible alumina relief - an equilibrium that is one supply interruption away from breaking upward. Norsk Hydro's Q2 results tomorrow (22-Jul, 07:00 CEST) are the next scheduled catalyst.

caveats: Report refreshed Tue 21-Jul-2026, ~06:05 London - BEFORE today's LME ring (official settlement 13:20 London). Westmetall had not yet published the 20-Jul official at refresh time, so the benchmark table carries the 17-Jul official (cash $3,154.00 / 3M $3,146.50 / stock 280,100 t). This is a publication-timing constraint, not a stale copy: the 20-Jul session is captured separately in the metals board using the publicly reported traded close.

MIXED SOURCE BASIS ON THE BOARD: aluminium, nickel, zinc and lead levels on the metals board are publicly reported traded prices (Trading Economics), while copper uses the LME's own day-delayed 3-month closing price. LME OFFICIAL settlements (the benchmark table and lme_series) come from Westmetall. Official, closing and traded prices are different reference points and will not tie out exactly - for 17-Jul, aluminium's 3M official was $3,146.50 while the LME 3M closing price was $3,185.00.

GEOPOLITICAL DEVELOPMENTS ARE FAST-MOVING and are NOT yet reflected in any published LME official. Monday's tanker interdictions, the third US fatality since Friday, and the strikes on Kuwaiti and Bahraini infrastructure all post-date the 17-Jul official. Treat the flat price as lagging the news flow.

PREMIUM ASSESSMENTS ARE PERIODIC, NOT DAILY: MJP settles quarterly, Rotterdam duty-unpaid and US Midwest are assessed by Platts/Fastmarkets on their own schedules. The figures shown are the latest PUBLIC prints (Rotterdam duty-unpaid mid $542.50/t and US Midwest 108.7 c/lb from the 03-Jul assessments; MJP Q3 settled $395/t on 06-Jul). They are not daily marks and should not be read as such.

ALL CONTENT IS PUBLIC MARKET INFORMATION for situational awareness only. No confidential, internal, company-specific or position data is included, and nothing here is trading, hedging or investment advice. Elliott Wave analysis is technical context, not a forecast or recommendation.

forward_path.basis: Latest PUBLISHED official (Westmetall); unchanged since 17-Jul (no session has traded). The cash–3M spread remains +$7.50 backwardation. Next official today, 20-Jul, 13:20 London.
Latest published official; unchanged since 17-Jul; front spread +$7.50 BACKWARDATION. Resistance $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382). Next official today, 13:20 London.

outlook.curve_note: The diagnostic has held steady through a weekend and overnight period that moved sharply against it. The cash–3M spread was last published at +$7.50 BACKWARDATION on the 17-Jul official — unchanged since Friday because no session has traded — on a 280,100 t stock base (−45.0% YTD). What has changed is the disruption the spread is pricing: overnight the US flew a 9th consecutive night of strikes on Iran, reinforced the theatre with F-16s (Germany) and F-35s (UK), and confirmed further US casualties (1 killed, 1 wounded, Iraq, 18-Jul, atop 2 KIA in Jordan, 17-Jul) — the war's US death toll now 17. The counterweight is still quantified at record scale — China exported 711 kt in June into a Q2 GDP print that missed consensus at 4.3% — but the swing factor has moved decisively toward renewed and deepening disruption rather than resolution. The 2026 consensus band ($3,200–3,500 averages) and the ~−365 kt structural deficit remain the floor beneath the argument; today's 13:20 London ring is the first chance for the curve to actually reprice the accumulated news.

consensus.note: Running above the World Bank base
First structural deficit in ~20 years; BofA flags near-term downside

balance.label: BofA/Morgan/BNP structural deficit; Goldman flags later surplus risk

~19% capacity in controlled shutdown through the Gulf disruption; restart path improves as US–Iran diplomacy advances
Output near the 45 Mt self-imposed cap; June unwrought-aluminium exports a RECORD 711 kt (+12.5% m/m; H1 3.4 Mt, +16.3% y/y); Q2 GDP 4.3% — production strong, domestic demand weak
capacity_pipeline.timing: Structural ceiling on output; record export flow

capacity_pipeline.change: New smelter capacity ramping (BofA flags as added supply)
Q2 production report (15-Jul): H1 CuEq +3%, aluminium guidance intact 3.25–3.45 Mt, bauxite recovering; AP60/Kitimat incremental low-carbon capacity

scenarios.drivers: Hormuz enforcement widens from crude tankers into general merchant traffic, or the Al Taweelah ramp stalls - either puts physical units at risk into a market with the lowest LME stocks since 2022 and no inventory buffer
drivers_list: Enforcement escalation: Iran disabled two tankers on 20-Jul; extension to general merchant traffic would expose roughly 9% of global primary supply
No inventory cushion: LME stock 280,100 t (lowest since 2022, about -45% YTD), SHFE drawing, cash-3M in backwardation
Energy: Brent above $90/bbl raises marginal costs and biases European/Asian capacity toward curtailment

scenarios.drivers: The current standoff persists - enforced but contained disruption at Hormuz offset by a steady Al Taweelah alumina ramp, leaving the flat price ranging between the early-July low near $3,085 and the $3,230-3,260 resistance stack
drivers_list: Al Taweelah ramps to 50% and beyond on schedule, capping alumina-driven cost pressure
Interdictions stay focused on crude rather than general cargo, keeping aluminium flows degraded but not halted
Backwardation persists but does not gap - prompt tight, forward balanced

scenarios.drivers: A credible diplomatic track reopens the strait while the Al Taweelah ramp completes ahead of schedule, releasing both the war premium and the alumina cost pressure into softening demand
drivers_list: Hormuz memorandum-of-understanding talks resume and shipping normalises, collapsing the war-risk premium
Alumina relief arrives faster than expected as Al Taweelah reaches full capability early
Demand destruction from sustained high prices plus the 50% Section 232 rate on US imports

risks.risk: Hormuz enforcement escalates: Iran disabled two tankers attempting transit on 20-Jul, the first direct interdiction of individual merchant vessels; extension from crude into general merchant traffic would put Gulf aluminium units (roughly 9% of global supply) directly at risk
Inventory floor: LME stock at 280,100 t is the lowest since 2022 and roughly -45% YTD with SHFE also drawing - there is no inventory buffer to absorb a fresh supply interruption
Energy cost squeeze: Brent above $90/bbl with EU gas following on a lag compresses marginal-producer margins in Europe and Asia and biases capacity toward curtailment rather than restart
Al Taweelah ramp disappoints or is interrupted - the alumina relief is the principal offset holding the flat price down and it is a ramp toward end-2026, not delivered tonnage
Demand destruction: sustained elevated prices plus the 50% Section 232 rate on US imports encourage substitution and scrap displacement, particularly in packaging and construction
Policy reversal risk in either direction - a Section 232 rate change or a credible Hormuz diplomatic track would reprice the premium complex sharply and quickly

catalysts.event: Norsk Hydro Q2 2026 results (webcast 08:30 CEST) - first European producer P&L covering a full quarter of the disruption regime; read on European smelter power costs and regional premium pass-through
Next LME official ring - the first official print able to reflect Monday's tanker interdictions; watch whether the cash-3M backwardation steepens beyond +$7.50
LME warehouse stock report - whether draws continue below 280,100 t, the lowest level since 2022
EGA Al Taweelah ramp from 50% of capacity toward full capability by end-2026 - the principal supply-side offset in the current balance
Q4-2026 MJP CIF Japan premium negotiations open against the decade-high Q3 settlement of $395/t
Strait of Hormuz transit volumes and whether interdictions extend from crude carriers into general merchant traffic

ai_analysis: The defining feature of this session is divergence: the flat price traded down to about $3,138/t (-0.85%) on 20-Jul at the same time as Iran physically disabled two tankers attempting to cross the Strait of Hormuz. Markets do not usually sell escalation of that kind unless something on the supply side is offsetting it - and here it is EGA's Al Taweelah alumina refinery, at full production since 10-Jul and ramping toward full capability by end-2026.

That makes the current balance conditional rather than settled. The structural tightness has not eased: LME stock at 280,100 t is the lowest since 2022 and roughly 45% below the start of the year, SHFE inventories are drawing, and cash-3M holds a +$7.50 backwardation. A market with those characteristics does not have inventory to absorb a fresh interruption. The alumina relief is the only slack in the system, and it is a ramp, not a delivered volume.

The risk skew is therefore asymmetric to the upside even though price action is soft. The two triggers to watch are any interruption to the Al Taweelah ramp, and any widening of the tanker interdictions from crude carriers into general merchant traffic - the latter would put physical aluminium units, not just sentiment, directly at risk on roughly 9% of global supply.

Regionally the premium complex is decoupled and should be read separately rather than as one signal: Japan's Q3 MJP settled at a decade-high $395/t on genuine scarcity, Rotterdam duty-unpaid has faded to $542.50/t as the war premium receded toward a structural floor of 6% duty plus CBAM, and the US duty-paid premium is dominated by the administered 50% Section 232 rate rather than by physical balance. Norsk Hydro's Q2 tomorrow is the next public data point on how these are flowing into producer margins.

premium_explainer.desc: The physical-delivery adder over the LME price: regional logistics, duties/tariffs and local tightness — quoted $/t (or ¢/lb in the US) on top of LME cash. It is what converts an exchange price into a delivered price.

Q3-2026 settled at $395/t CIF Japan (Platts, 06-Jul) — a decade high and the fourth straight rise ($86 → $195 → $351.5 → $395). SMM flags spot tightness and squeeze risk under the LME's deep backwardation, firming CIF spot toward ~$365. Q4 talks (~Sep) open against a decade-high anchor and a still-disrupted strait — with US–Iran diplomacy (MoU to ~18-Aug) the swing factor.

Platts duty-unpaid last publicly marked ~$525–560 (mid $542.50, 03-Jul). The 6% EU duty, CBAM and Mozal's care & maintenance keep the structural floor; the war-risk freight component is easing at the margin as the US–Iran conflict de-escalates. The prospective 20% strait toll died on 14-Jul.

SMM's weekly DDP print ~109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul); Platts MW Transaction last printed 108.7¢ (03-Jul). The 50% Section-232 tariff-$ leg (≈$1,577 at the 17-Jul LME cash) dominates the all-in — US premia track Washington and the LME, not local tightness. Alcoa Q2 confirmed the tariff as a ~$35m q/q cost on its Canadian metal.

The decomposition below separates the mechanical tariff leg (Section-232 rate × LME) from the residual market premium. Most of the US 'premium' is a policy transfer, not a scarcity signal — and the new 20% strait toll extends the same logic to the freight leg of any Gulf-origin cargo, US-bound or not.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): Q3 settled $395 (decade high, unchanged); CIF spot ~$365 on squeeze risk under the LME's deep backwardation. The overnight escalation (9th consecutive night of strikes, further US casualties, Strait transits near a standstill) strengthens the case for Q4 talks (~Sep) to open at or above the $395 anchor rather than fade toward the lower half of the $340–410 range.

European (EDU): last public Platts mark ~$525–560 (03-Jul), unchanged. The 6% duty + CBAM floor plus Mozal's outage keep Europe structurally tighter than pre-war; the marginal driver — Gulf war-risk freight — continues to widen rather than ease.

US Midwest: SMM DDP ~109.75¢/lb (10-Jul), unchanged. The tariff leg (50% × LME ≈ $1,577 at the 17-Jul cash) is ~two-thirds of the all-in, so US premia track the LME and Washington more than Gulf events directly — but a sustained, further-escalating Gulf disruption raises input-cost and logistics risk for any US-bound metal that transits affected routes. Watch the next weekly print, not the spot survey.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$68 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); oversupply persists — Metro Mining shipped a record 1.8 Mt in Q2 (+7% y/y; 2026 guidance 6.6–7.1 Mt held). Guinea is reportedly considering capping 2026 exports near 150 Mt (vs 178 Mt shipped in 2025, a ~15.7% cut) targeting >$100/dmt, but no formal decree had been issued as of end-June; no fresher public print surfaced over the weekend.

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst/ChemTradeAsia); no fresher public print over the weekend.

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent); no fresher public print over the weekend.

alumina_outlook: Alumina: surplus intact and deepening — EGA's restart ramps through H2, SHFE-adjacent SMM Alumina Index eased to ¥2,730.17/t (−¥12.8 w/w), and ex-China consolidation has stepped down to $300–330 (FOB Australia $328, API ~$303, Platts futures ~$308). SMM's June ex-China output dip (−6% y/y) and Guinea's still-undecreed quota push are the counterweights to watch.

Bauxite: Guinea oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38); record Australian shipments add length. Guinea is reportedly weighing a 2026 export cap near 150 Mt (vs 178 Mt in 2025) to defend a >$100/dmt target, but had issued no formal decree as of end-June.

Caustic soda / lime / freight: caustic stable (~$500/t FOB NE Asia). The delivered-cost leg has moved further the wrong way overnight — a 9th consecutive night of US strikes on Iran, a second consecutive strike on Kuwait's desalination/power infrastructure, and Strait transits down to as few as ~3 vessels/24h mean war-risk cover on Gulf-bound reagent and alumina freight is wider again, not fading. The commodity is still cheap; insuring and moving it through the Gulf is more expensive and less certain than at any point since the conflict began.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen. Energy complex firm: Brent broke above $90/bbl in early Monday trade (highest since mid-June) on the further-escalated US-Iran conflict; EU TTF gas ~€53/MWh — gas-indexed smelter power follows with a lag. Rising carbon-cost exposure.

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-transparent leg of the input basket — Cu 3M $13,606.00 (16-Jul official, latest published); the complex firmed through the week (Cu +8.8% YTD). Busbar/cathode exposure prices off the LME.

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.note: Overnight the US flew a 9th consecutive night of strikes on Iran and is reinforcing with F-16s (Germany) and F-35s (UK). Wire reporting puts Strait transits at as few as ~3 vessels per 24 hours, versus a normal ~110/day pre-war; the IRGC claims to have intercepted 4 further vessels. ~80–85% of Gulf aluminium exports and ~60% of regional alumina imports use this waterway.

logistics.name: Southern (Omani) corridor
logistics.val: Not verified reopened; risk further widened
logistics.note: The workaround corridor discussed in prior weeks has not materialised into confirmed reopening. With Oman among the states struck in recent days and the conflict escalating (9th night of strikes, mounting US casualties), the corridor's near-term viability looks weaker than at any point since it was first floated.

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.val: Sharply wider — escalation continuing, not fading
logistics.note: Lloyd's List and the Hormuz Strait Monitor have cited war-risk quotes around $10–14m per voyage on some routes, with rates near 2.5% and up to ~5% for US/UK/Israel-linked tonnage. With a 9th consecutive night of strikes, further US casualties, and repeated hits on Kuwaiti energy/water infrastructure, retain elevated insurance, interdiction and deviation-risk assumptions in all Gulf-routed freight and delivered-cost models.

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.val: 2,752 (17-Jul close, −3.1% d/d) — latest published, today's print pending
logistics.note: Last published at 2,752 on 17-Jul (−3.10% d/d, third straight down session), down more than 6% on the week — dry-bulk demand softening even as the Gulf war-risk premium (a separate, faster-moving cost line) continues to build. Today's session print was not yet available at this report's refresh; price the two legs (dry-bulk freight vs. war-risk surcharge) separately (Trading Economics/Hellenic Shipping News/Baltic Exchange).

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: With Strait transits near a standstill and the conflict escalating rather than cooling (9th night of strikes, further US casualties), overland trucking to Red Sea ports remains a more actively-used contingency than earlier in the conflict. Keep carrier capacity, costings and lead times current for any Gulf-origin cargo.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: Repeated strikes on Kuwaiti and other Gulf infrastructure, plus mounting US casualties, keep force-majeure and deviation clauses firmly live for any Gulf-routed cargo; war-risk surcharge pass-through and interdiction/delay risk are live, worsening contract points to negotiate cargo-by-cargo. Generic supply-chain observation, not company-specific.

inputs_summary: Track closely (material and tradeable): power - Brent topped $90/bbl on Monday as the conflict intensified, with EU gas exposure following on a lag; calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency); silicon and magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated); copper (LME-transparent, 3M day-delayed close $13,525.50 on 20-Jul, -0.54%).

Easing for the first time since March: alumina. EGA's Al Taweelah refinery has been at full production since 10-Jul, targeting 50% of plant capacity within days and full capability by end-2026. At 2.4 Mt in 2025 it covered close to half of EGA's alumina requirement. This is the single most important input-cost development in the current picture.
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B - no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.
Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (roughly 85% China), CPC, and Gulf-routed material now exposed to enforced rather than merely threatened interdiction at Hormuz.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: LONG TERM (weekly / position). The count remains a completed five-wave impulse from the 2,300 base into the 3,855 high of 02-Jun-2026 - wave 5 extended, as commodities typically do - followed by a corrective A-B-C. Wave A fell to 3,359, B recovered to 3,406, and C bottomed at 3,061.50 on 02-Jul, holding the .50 retracement at 3,077 within a target zone of 2,950-3,110 defined by the confluence of the .50 level and the prior fourth wave of lesser degree. All three cardinal rules remain satisfied on the impulse: wave 2 did not retrace beyond 100% of wave 1, wave 3 is not the shortest, and wave 4 does not overlap wave 1. BASE CASE (55%): the C low is in and price is building a corrective base beneath the .382 trigger at 3,260, with the 20-Jul softness to about $3,138 a normal retest rather than a new leg down. A sustained break above 3,260 opens 3,450. BULL ALTERNATE (30%): the correction ended at 3,061.50 and a new impulse is forming - a decisive close above 3,260 with the backwardation steepening would confirm, targeting 3,620-3,800. BEAR ALTERNATE (15%): the C leg is extending as a deeper zigzag toward the .618 at 2,894. INVALIDATION: a weekly close below 3,061.50 negates the completed-C reading and shifts the count to the bear alternate; a weekly close above 3,406 (the B high) negates the bear case entirely. CONFIDENCE: moderate - the .50 hold and the persistent backwardation support the base case, but the divergence between softening price and escalating supply risk argues for tighter-than-usual invalidation discipline. Technical context only, not advice.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: SHORT TERM (daily / swing). The corrective sequence off the 3,855 high of 02-Jun counts as A to 3,359, B to 3,607 (the spike that was sold), and C to 3,065 on 02-Jul, which held the .50 retracement. Since then price has been carving a shallow base: the early-July low near 3,085, a rebound that stalled below the 3,230 MA20, and a 20-Jul session back down to about 3,138. Because wave A was a five-wave move, alternation argues the correction resolves as a zigzag rather than a flat, and the shallow, overlapping recovery since 02-Jul is consistent with base-building rather than impulsive advance. BASE CASE (55%): the base holds and price grinds toward the 3,230 MA20 and then the .382 at 3,260. BULL ALTERNATE (30%): a gap higher on Hormuz enforcement news clears 3,260 directly - the setup is asymmetric given LME stocks at the lowest since 2022 and a backwarded curve. BEAR ALTERNATE (15%): failure at 3,138 opens a retest of 3,085 and then 3,061.50. INVALIDATION: a daily close below 3,061.50 voids the base-building count; a daily close above 3,260 voids the bear alternate. Watch RSI for divergence at the 3,230-3,260 resistance stack and whether the cash-3M backwardation steepens on the next official - curve confirmation is the tell. CONFIDENCE: moderate. Technical context only, not advice.

premium_drivers.t: Hormuz: enforcement, not just threat - the dominant driver
premium_drivers.d: Iran disabled two oil tankers attempting to transit the Strait of Hormuz without permission on Monday 20-Jul, the first direct interdiction of individual merchant vessels in this conflict. A third US service member was killed since Friday, US strikes reached a ninth consecutive day, and Iran struck Kuwaiti power/desalination sites and Bahrain. With roughly 9% of global primary aluminium supply originating in the Gulf, war-risk cover and routing costs are building rather than fading.
src: NPR - U.S. and Iran launch new strikes after 3 U.S. service members are killed (20-Jul-2026)

premium_drivers.t: US Section 232 at 50% - the administered floor under the US duty-paid premium
premium_drivers.d: Products made entirely or almost entirely of aluminium pay 50% on their full customs value under the proclamation signed 01-Jun-2026, effective 08-Jun-2026 through 31-Dec-2027, with CBP guidance issued 05-Jun-2026 (CSMS #68855869). At an LME around $3,150/t this is roughly $1,575/t of pure policy cost embedded in US duty-paid metal, mechanically decoupling the US premium from the rest of the world.
src: U.S. Customs and Border Protection - Section 232 Tariffs on Steel and Aluminum FAQs

premium_drivers.t: Europe: war premium fading against a structural floor
premium_drivers.d: Platts assessed duty-unpaid P1020A in-warehouse Rotterdam at $525-560/t (mid $542.50) on 03-Jul, down from $600-625/t on 13-May as the Middle East war premium receded. The floor beneath it is structural rather than sentiment-driven: the 6% EU import duty, CBAM, and idled European capacity. Liquidity remains thin as Rotterdam competes with Asia for units.
src: S&P Global - TRADE REVIEW: European aluminum market seeks floor as Middle East war premium fades (07-Jul-2026)

premium_drivers.t: Japan: Q3 settled at a decade high, Q4 negotiations open against it
premium_drivers.d: The Q3-2026 MJP CIF Japan premium settled at $395/t (assessed 06-Jul), a decade high and roughly 12% above Q2's $350-353/t. Q4 talks open around September against that anchor; the swing variable is whether Hormuz disruption persists at current intensity or a diplomatic track restores flow.
src: S&P Global Platts Q3-2026 MJP settlement $395/t, via IndexBox

premium_settlement_src: S&P Global Platts Q3-2026 MJP settlement $395/t (assessed 06-Jul-2026), via IndexBox

so_what.line: Iran disabled two oil tankers that tried to transit the Strait of Hormuz without its permission on Monday - the first direct interdiction of individual merchant vessels in this conflict - which turns a de-facto shipping stoppage into an enforced one, even as the aluminium flat price slipped to about $3,138/t on the 20-Jul session.
points: What moved: the 20-Jul session traded aluminium down to roughly $3,138/t (-0.85%), giving back part of the rebound off the early-July four-month low near $3,085 (Trading Economics). Note the LME official for 20-Jul had not yet been published by Westmetall at this ~06:05 London refresh, so the benchmark table still carries the 17-Jul official (cash $3,154.00 / 3M $3,146.50).

Why it matters: Iran's disabling of two tankers on Monday escalates Hormuz from blockade-by-threat to blockade-by-enforcement. The Gulf accounts for roughly 9% of global primary aluminium supply, so shipping risk now sits directly on the physical supply line rather than only on sentiment.

The offset: EGA's Al Taweelah refinery resumed full alumina production on 10-Jul and is ramping toward full capability by end-2026 - the only genuine supply-side relief in the picture, and the reason the flat price is drifting rather than spiking despite the escalation.

Watch this week: Norsk Hydro's Q2 results tomorrow (22-Jul, 07:00 CEST) - the first European producer P&L covering the full disruption quarter; whether LME stocks continue drawing from 280,100 t; and whether the cash-3M backwardation widens when the official curve next prints.
